speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Schillinger, you were just telling us about your children. Let's recapitulate a little bit. And which ones of them are married at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don, my oldest son. He's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom did marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's married-- Karen Klein, who was born in Mallorca, in Spain. And her father is a Polish Jew who-- just to make things more complicated-- as a child was sent to Hungary from Poland, where he was hidden and survived. So he speaks a little Hungarian also. And Karen's mother is a Yemenite Israeli woman, a wonderful lady. And Karen spent some time in Israel also, two or three years, where she was educated, too. So she speaks Spanish-- mother tongue-- French, English, and Hebrew, and all that fluently. She went to Georgetown University. And she graduated in foreign service.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are the other sons married also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dean, who is a doctor in San Francisco, married to Ariella Heichman, whose mother is Israeli, was a pianist. And father is a conductor in New Jersey. And Ariella is a lawyer who works in legal aids in San Francisco for battered women. She's also a very bright woman. And I'm extremely lucky with my daughter-in-laws.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your third son?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my third is Ben in Philadelphia. He's not married yet. But he has a, a steady girlfriend whose name is [? Remy. ?] And that's what I want to say that, that Don, my oldest, he runs the budget for the medical school in the University of Pennsylvania. He's a computer scientist.

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds like you're rather proud of your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And Brown University had an important part in our lives. Because Dean went to Brown. And he played oboe in the Brown symphony. And his wife now, Ariella, played first cello in that orchestra. So the two met there and ended up in a very happy marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Knowing that the tape of, of this interview is-- we hope-- going to be around for a very long time and will be seen by researchers and scholars perhaps well into the future, if there were any message that you could give to them and to your children and grandchildren and great-grandchildren someday who will also have an opportunity to see this tape, what message would you convey to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think of two messages. One is in the darkest and most horrible times of your life, there is always some hope, some, some support that you can find. And this support is in your family, number one. I feel that the reason I survived-- besides the American army-- is my family, my family's help. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after all this happenings, what I see is the optimism what I gain and the, and the wonderful feeling by, by looking at my children, their wives, and new generations to come, that I can see the continuation of talents as I can see that my children play musical instruments, which came from grandma and grandpa from both sides, that had some artistic talents that came from grandpa from one side, that one continues my profession. So it made it worthwhile for me to survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is one message. The other one is in the worst of times [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the worst of people with-- amongst the more brutal people, you will always find good people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not to lose hope, not to lose faith in humanity. I described quite a few wonderful, righteous Christians. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Schillinger, can you describe this document for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document was put together by my father. And this is the family tree from his side. And it begins with Mendel Schillinger, who was born sometime in the 18th century. I don't know the exact date. But I understand our family reaches way back 200 to 300 years. And his son was Abraham, who was born in 1800. And his son, my grandfather, was Jacob. And he was born in 1838 in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this, this genealogy indicates that your family had been in your home town at least from the 18th-- sometime in the 18th century.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the same village, but in the same--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Larger area.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same province.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This photograph is of my grandfather, Henry Diamond, who was born in Czechoslovakia in last century and emigrated to the United States in 1906. And since my mother was a very little girl, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] almost an infant, she, she was left behind in Czechoslovakia. And my grandpa settled in Flatbush in Brooklyn with his wife, whose name was Ernestine Klein, my grandmother. And they lived here until he passed away at age 86. This is my father's motorcycle, which I loved. From left to right is my sister, Eva, my mother, Lola, and myself, and a family friend. And we are on an outing next to the river in Szeghalom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when would this have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately 1932, '31 and a half. This is a formal photograph of my sister, Eva, and myself. I must have been maybe four years old. And she was nine. This is my father, Joseph. He was standing in the terrace of our home in Szeghalom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when would that have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 1950s or the late '40s. That lady is Marcella. And she was our housekeeper, my beloved Marcella, who offered to hide us and save us from the Germans. She saved many material possessions for us. And for many, many years, she worked in our house and, later on, in my father's store as well. I think I am about six years old, and my dog, Bungy. That photograph was taken in an outing of my Jewish school in a little forest in Szeghalom. And the children are from, left to right, Judith Kiss, the first one-- died in Auschwitz. The next boy, I don't remember his name. But he died in Auschwitz. Then I am sitting there with a knife in my hand in the foreground. Behind me, George Magyar, who is a physicist in London. Next to it standing is Georgie Herman, the boy whom I beat up. And I discovered he had a circumcision. In front of him-- Georgie died in Auschwitz as well. And in front of him is Koka Weiss. And Weiss-- I don't remember what happened to him. And next to him, the boy who was raising his hand, a blond boy, is John Werner. And he is a professor of engineering in Stockholm. He was the best friend-- my best friend. And he remained my best friend. And his parents were my parents' best friends. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Next to him is our teacher, whose name was Weinberger. And he became, as I mentioned before, a cancer researcher, a famous doctor in Norway after the war. Next to him on the extreme right is Tibi Rohmer. And he died in Auschwitz as well. This is a high school picture of the soccer team, which was taken after the war. And I, I am sitting in the foreground on the right side. In the background, you can see the church steeple where our friend, the minister, worked. This is Dr. Julius Rock, a physician and a major in the 30th Infantry Unit of the 9th Army. That train is where we were liberated by his unit and by him. And he, afterwards, became a gynecologist in Rochester, New York. I'm forever thankful to him. This is part of our family. From left to right, it's me. And that's Dean, who is in San Francisco, Zahava, my wife, and Ben, who is in Philadelphia now. This photograph was taken in Israel at the wedding of my oldest son, Don, and his wife, Karen, Karen Klein. That also was my, my son's wedding, which was performed in, in a Yemenite fashion and with Yemenite traditions, as you can see from the outfits. We were all dressed up like this. And it was extremely exciting, colorful, and wonderfully warm with all our new Yemenite relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date was in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, it was June.

speaker: interviewee
text: June of 19-- June 6, 1996. This lovely couple is Dean and his wife, Ariella Heichman. And she's a lawyer in San Francisco, a very lovely, talented girl who also be a great musician.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Schillinger, I'd like you to introduce the young lady who is sitting on your-- to your left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is the most pleasant point of my interview to introduce my wife, Zahava. We were-- we have been married for 36 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to thank our children for being instrumental in allowing George to tell his sad story. I'm sure that we will all sit and you look at this period of his life in the near future and share some of the sad moments. We have all shared a lot of happy ones since.

speaker: interviewee
text: We would like to thank you and Mr Spielberg for the opportunity of giving witness to what happened. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: We want to thank you.
